name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤٨)
```

```english
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, should you be truthful?’ (48)
```

```arabic
مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ (٤٩)
```

```english
They do not await but a single Cry that will seize them as they wrangle. (49)
```

```arabic
فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٥٠)
```

```english
Then they will not be able to make any will, nor will they return to their folks. (50)
```

```arabic
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنسِلُونَ (٥١)
```

```english
And when the Trumpet is blown, behold, there they will be, scrambling towards their Lord from their graves! (51)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا مَنۢ بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا ۜ ۗ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَصَدَقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٥٢)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep?’ ‘This is what the All-beneficent had promised, and the apostles had spoken the truth!’ (52)
```

```arabic
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (٥٣)
```

```english
It will be but a single Cry, and, behold, they will all be presented before Us! (53)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٤)
```

```english
‘Today no soul will be wronged in the least, nor will you be requited except for what you used to do.’ (54)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى شُغُلٍ فَـٰكِهُونَ (٥٥)
```

```english
Indeed today the inhabitants of paradise rejoice in their engagements (55)
```

```arabic
هُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُمْ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ مُتَّكِـُٔونَ (٥٦)
```

```english
—they and their mates, reclining on couches in the shades. (56)
```

```arabic
لَهُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَلَهُم مَّا يَدَّعُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
There they have fruits, and they have whatever they want. (57)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْلًا مِّن رَّبٍّ رَّحِيمٍ (٥٨)
```

```english
‘Peace!’—a watchword from the all-merciful Lord. (58)
```

```arabic
وَٱمْتَـٰزُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
And ‘Get apart today, you guilty ones!’ (59)
```

```arabic
۞ أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٦٠)
```

```english
‘Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. (60)
```

```arabic
وَأَنِ ٱعْبُدُونِى ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦١)
```

```english
Worship Me. That is a straight path”? (61)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا ۖ أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Certainly, he has led astray many of your generations. Have you not exercised your reason? (62)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
This is the hell you had been promised! (63)
```

```arabic
ٱصْلَوْهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Enter it today, because of what you used to deny. (64)
```

```arabic
ٱلْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلَىٰٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنَآ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness concerning what they used to earn.’ (65)
```

## (Commentary)

Having concluded the detailed exposition of the verses of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — which were referred to summarily at the beginning of the discourse, He begins the detailed account of the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of creation to Allah for judgment*. He mentions the manner in which the Hour will commence, the gathering of people for reckoning (الحساب) and recompense (الجزاء), and what the inhabitants of Paradise and the criminals will be rewarded with. All of this serves as an elucidation of the previously summarized report concerning the Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** "They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’" (36:48). This is a statement from them issued by way of mockery and based on denial. Perhaps for this reason, the demonstrative pronoun for the near (هذا) was used, and because the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers frequently spoke to them about the Day of Resurrection and warned them of it. The term **promise** (الوعد) is used for both good and evil when mentioned alone; however, when contrasted with **threat** (الوعيد), "promise" (الوعد) is specified for good and "threat" (الوعيد) for evil.

His saying, the Exalted: **ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ** "They do not await but a single shout that will seize them while they are [still] disputing." (36:49). **Awaiting** (النظر) here carries the meaning of waiting (الانتظار). What is meant by the "shout" (الصيحة) is the first blast of the Trumpet, as aided by the context. Describing the shout as "single" (واحدة) points to the insignificance of their affair to Allah—exalted is His greatness—as it requires no additional effort. The word **يَخِصِّمُونَ** is originally *yakhtasimun* (يختصمون), derived from disputation (الاختصام), meaning arguing and quarreling.

The verse is a response to their saying: "When will this promise be fulfilled?" It is phrased in a manner that mocks them and belittles their situation, just as their own statement was. The meaning is: these speakers who ask "When will this promise be fulfilled?"—a question that implies they are waiting—await nothing but a single shout, which is easy for Us without effort or burden. It will seize them such that they will have no capacity to flee or escape from it, while they are heedless of it, disputing among themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلا إِلى أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** "Then they will not be able to make any bequest, nor will they return to their families." (36:50). That is, it follows from this shout—inasmuch as it surprises them and grants them no respite—that they die instantly. Thus, they cannot make a bequest (توصية)—especially since death encompasses them all at once, leaving no one to whom a bequest could be made—nor can they return to their families if they were, for example, outside their homes.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ** "And the Trumpet will be blown, and behold, they will be rushing from the graves toward their Lord." (36:51). This is the second blast of the Trumpet, by which life and resurrection (البعث) occur. **Graves** (الأجداث) is the plural of *jadath* (جدث), meaning the tomb. **Rushing** (النسل) means walking quickly. Expressing it as "toward their Lord" (إِلى رَبِّهِمْ) is a rebuke to them because they used to deny His Lordship (الربوبية). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا هذا ما وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** "They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep? This is what the All-beneficent promised, and the apostles spoke the truth!’" (36:52). **Raising** (البعث) means making one stand up. **Place of sleep** (المرقد) refers to the place of slumber, meaning the grave. Their referring to Him, the Exalted, as "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is a form of seeking mercy, whereas in the world they used to say: "And what is the All-Beneficent?" (25:60). His saying: "and the apostles spoke the truth" is conjoined to His saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised"; a verbal sentence may be conjoined to a nominal one.

Their saying: "Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep?" is based on their denial of the Resurrection while they were in the world, and the deep-rooted effect of that denial and heedlessness regarding the Day of Recompense in their souls. They remain immersed in their desires; thus, when they rise from their graves rushing toward the Gathering (المحشر), they are suddenly confronted with entry into a realm where nothing awaits them but the expectation of evil. They are seized by the Great Terror (الفزع الأكبر) and a bewilderment that even mountains could not withstand. Therefore, they first hasten to cry out for woe and destruction, as was their habit in the world when falling into perils. Then they ask who raised them from their place of sleep because the bewilderment surrounding them made them oblivious to everything.

Then they recall what the Messengers (عليه‌السلام) used to remind them of regarding the true promise of Resurrection and Recompense. They testify to the truth of the promise and seek refuge in mercy, saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised," following their habit in the world where they would flatter their enemy if he overcame them, showing humiliation and admitting to wrongdoing and negligence. Then they confirm the Messengers by saying: "and the apostles spoke the truth."

From what has preceded, the reason for their cry of woe upon being raised becomes clear. Secondly, the reason for their question about who raised them from their place of sleep—which suggests they were initially ignorant of it—becomes clear, followed by their admission that it is what the All-Beneficent promised and their confirmation of the Messengers in what they conveyed from Him, the Exalted. It also becomes clear that the saying: "Who raised us from our place of sleep..." etc., and the saying: "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." etc., are both part of their speech.

It has been said that the phrase "and the apostles spoke the truth" is conjoined to the object of "what" (ما), where "what" is either a relative pronoun or an infinitive-forming particle, and that "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." etc., is a response from Allah, the angels, or the believers to their question: "Who raised us from our place of sleep?" It is not hidden that this is contrary to the apparent meaning, especially if "what" (ما) is taken as an infinitive-forming particle. Furthermore, if the phrase "This is what the All-Beneficent promised..." were a response from Allah or the angels to their question "Who raised us from our place of sleep?", the response would have identified the agent (the doer) rather than the act, because they asked about the agent of the raising! The claim that the shift was intended to remind them of their disbelief and rebuke them for it, while implicitly pointing to the agent, is of no significant value.

It also becomes clear that "This is what the All-Beneficent promised" consists of a subject and a predicate. It has been said that "this" (هذا) is an adjective for "our place of sleep" (مرقدنا) by interpreting the demonstrative pronoun as a derivative, and that "what" (ما) is a subject whose predicate is omitted, estimated as "what the All-Beneficent promised is true." This is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ كانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً فَإِذا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنا مُحْضَرُونَ** "It will be but a single shout, and behold, they will all be brought before Us." (36:53). The subject of *kānat* (كانت) is omitted, and the estimation is: "The event or the blast was nothing but a single blast," which surprises them such that they are gathered and brought before Us without delay or respite. The expression "before Us" (لَدَيْنا) is used because the day is the Day of Presence for the final judgment before Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَالْيَوْمَ لا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً وَلا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** "Today no soul shall be wronged in the least, nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do." (36:54). That is, on this day, He judges between them with a just judgment and decrees with a truthful decree; thus, no soul shall be wronged in the least.

His saying: "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" is an explanatory conjunction to His saying: "Today no soul shall be wronged in the least." It is, in reality, a demonstrative proof for the negation of injustice on that day, as it indicates that the recompense for the deeds of the doers on that day is the very deeds themselves. Injustice (الظلم) — *the act of placing something in other than its proper place* — is inconceivable in such a case, because burdening the doer with his own deed is necessarily placing a thing in its proper place.

The address in the verse is by way of representing the Day of Resurrection and making it and those within it present through linguistic attention. It is not—as some have imagined—a narration of what will be said to them or how they will be addressed by Allah, the Glorified, the angels, or the believers on the Day of Resurrection; there is no requirement for this from the context. The addressees of His saying: "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" are both the felicitous and the wretched.

As for the objection that the restriction (الحصر) precludes generalization—since He, the Exalted, pays the believers their rewards in full and increases them manifold from His grace—it is refuted by the fact that the restriction in the verse pertains to the recompense and reward for the deed itself. Verses indicating an "increase," such as His saying: "They will have whatever they wish therein, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35), refer to a matter beyond recompense and reward, outside the scope of the deed.

It has also been answered that the meaning of the verse is that the righteous person's reward will not be diminished, and the wicked person's punishment will not be increased, as wisdom would contradict that. However, increasing the reward or decreasing the punishment is not prohibited. Alternatively, the meaning of "nor shall you be requited except for what you used to do" is that you will only be requited with the *kind* of your deed: if good, then good; if evil, then evil. However, if the meaning of the verse were as mentioned, the problem would be resolved, but the issue lies in whether the verse actually indicates that.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ الْيَوْمَ فِي شُغُلٍ فاكِهُونَ** "Indeed today the inhabitants of paradise are happily occupied." (36:55). **Occupation** (الشغل) is an affair that occupies a person and diverts them from everything else. **Happy** (الفاكه) is derived from *fakāhah* (الفكاهة), which is talking about what pleases or enjoying and taking delight; it has no triliteral root verb, according to what has been said.

It has been said that **فاكِهُونَ** means "possessors of fruit" (ذوو فاكهة), similar to *lābin* (possessor of milk) and *tāmir* (possessor of dates). This is unlikely because fruit (الفاكهة) is mentioned in the context, and there is no reason for its repetition. The meaning is that the inhabitants of Paradise on this day are in an affair that occupies them from everything else, which is the enjoyment of Paradise, taking delight therein.

His saying, the Exalted: **هُمْ وَأَزْواجُهُمْ فِي ظِلالٍ عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ مُتَّكِؤُنَ** "They and their spouses will be in shades, reclining on couches." (36:56). **Shades** (الظلال) is the plural of *zill* (ظل), and it is said to be the plural of *zullah* (ظلة), which is a covering from the sun, whether a roof, trees, or otherwise. **Couch** (الأريكة) is anything one reclines upon, such as a cushion or similar. The meaning is: they—the inhabitants of Paradise—and their spouses from their believing wives in the world or from the *Hūr al-ʿĪn*, are in shades or coverings from the sun and other things, reclining on couches as the reclining of the honored.

His saying, the Exalted: **لَهُمْ فِيها فاكِهَةٌ وَلَهُمْ ما يَدَّعُونَ** "There they will have fruits and they will have whatever they call for." (36:57). **Fruit** (الفاكهة) refers to the fruits one enjoys, such as apples, citrons, and the like. His saying: **يَدَّعُونَ** is from *iddiʿā’* (الادعاء) in the sense of wishing; meaning, they have in Paradise fruit and they have therein whatever they wish for and request.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَلامٌ قَوْلاً مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ** "‘Peace!’—a watchword from a merciful Lord." (36:58). "Peace" (سلام) is a subject whose predicate is omitted, and the indefiniteness is for magnification (التفخيم); the estimation is "Peace be upon them" or "For them is peace." "A watchword" (قَوْلاً) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, the estimation being: "I say it as a word from a Merciful Lord."

It is apparent that the "Peace" is from Him, the Exalted, and it is other than the peace of the angels mentioned in His saying: "and the angels will enter upon them from every gate: ‘Peace be to you, for your patience.’ How excellent is the ultimate abode!" (13:24).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** "‘Get apart today, O you guilty ones!’" (36:59). That is, We say today to the criminals: "Separate yourselves from the inhabitants of Paradise." This is their segregation from them on the Day of Resurrection and the fulfillment of what He said in another place: "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:28), and His saying: "Do those who commit vices suppose that We shall make them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal?" (45:21).

His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ** "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy." (36:60). **Exhortation** (العهد) means a command or testament. What is meant by the "worship of Satan" is obeying him in what he whispers and commands, for there is no obedience except to Allah or to one whose obedience He has commanded. The prohibition of obeying him is justified by his being a manifest enemy, for an enemy does not desire good for his foe.

It has been said that what is meant by his worship is the worship of idols besides Allah, and it is attributed to Satan because it occurs through his enticement and beautification; however, this is an unnecessary affectation. The address is directed to the criminals with the title "Children of Adam" because the enmity of Satan first took root with Adam when he was commanded to prostrate to him but refused and was arrogant, thus being stoned. He then returned to his progeny with enmity and threatened them, as Allah, the Exalted, narrated when he said: "He said, ‘Do You see this one whom You have honored above me? If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, [all] but a few.’" (17:62).

As for His covenant (العهد) and testament to the Children of Adam not to obey him, it is what He exhorted them with through the tongues of His Messengers and Prophets, warning them against following him, such as His saying: "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you as he expelled your parents from paradise" (7:27), and His saying: "And do not let Satan bar you [from the right path]. Indeed he is your manifest enemy." (43:62).

It has been said that what is meant by the covenant is His covenant with them in the Realm of Particles (عالم الذر) when He said: "‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes.’" (7:172). You have already learned from what we presented in the commentary on the verse of the Particles that the covenant there is, in one respect, the very same covenant directed to them in the world.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** "‘And worship Me. This is a straight path’?" (36:61). This is an explanatory conjunction to what preceded it. Discussion regarding the meaning of the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — has already passed in the commentary on His saying: "Guide us on the straight path" in Surah al-Fatihah.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنْكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيراً أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْقِلُونَ** "He has certainly led astray a great multitude of you. Did you not use to apply reason?" (36:62). **Multitude** (الجبل) means a group, and it is said to mean a large group. The speech is based on rebuke and censure.

His saying, the Exalted: **هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ** "This is the hell you were promised." (36:63). That is, the threat of it continued to be issued to you time after time through the tongues of the Prophets and Messengers (عليه‌السلام). The first time Allah, the Glorified, threatened with it was when He said to Iblis: "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all." (15:42-43). The wording of the verse points to the bringing forth of Hell on that day.

His saying, the Exalted: **اصْلَوْهَا الْيَوْمَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ** "Enter it today because of your disbelief." (36:64). **Entering** (الصلا) means remaining and following; it is also said to mean enduring heat. It appears from His saying: "because of your disbelief" that the address is to the disbelievers, and they are the ones meant by "the guilty ones" (المجرمين).

His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** "Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness to what they used to earn." (36:65). That is, each of them will bear witness to what they used to earn through its medium: the hands regarding the sins they committed with them, and the feet regarding the sins specific to them, as the context suggests.

From here it becomes clear that every limb will speak regarding the deed specific to it, and that the mention of hands and feet is by way of example. For this reason, in another place, hearing, sight, and the heart are mentioned, as in Surah al-Isra, verse 36. In another place, the skins are mentioned, as in Surah Fussilat, verse 20. Some related discussion will follow in the commentary on Surah Fussilat, if Allah wills.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ما يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً** — the verse — he said: "That is at the end of time; a shout will be cried out among them—while they are in their markets disputing—and they will all die in their places. Not one of them will return to his home, nor will he make a bequest. That is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلا إِلى أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ**."

In *al-Majmaʿ*, in a hadith: **تقوم الساعة والرجلان قد نشرا ثوبهما يتبايعان ـ فما يطويانه حتى تقوم الساعة ، والرجل يرفع أكلته إلى فيه حتى تقوم الساعة ، والرجل يليط حوضه ليسقي ماشيته ـ فما يسقيها حتى تقوم** — "The Hour will commence while two men have spread out their garment to trade—they will not fold it before the Hour commences. A man will raise his morsel to his mouth, and the Hour will commence. A man will be plastering (يليط) his trough to water his livestock—he will not water them before it commences."

I say: This meaning is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and likewise from Qatadah from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a disconnected (مرسلا) report.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَإِذا هُمْ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ إِلى رَبِّهِمْ يَنْسِلُونَ** — he said: "From the graves." In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا** — "The people were in the graves; when they rose, they thought they had been asleep and said: **يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا**. The angels said: **هذا ما وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ**."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Abu Dharr (may Allah have mercy on him) used to say in his sermon: 'Between death and resurrection is but like a sleep you slept and then woke up from.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ الْيَوْمَ فِي شُغُلٍ فاكِهُونَ** — he said: "They joke with women and play with them."

In the same, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **فِي ظِلالٍ عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ مُتَّكِؤُنَ** — "The couches (الأرائك) are beds with canopies over them."

In the same, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **سَلامٌ قَوْلاً مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ** — he said: "The Peace from Him is security (الأمان)." Regarding His saying: **وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — he said: "When Allah gathers the creation on the Day of Resurrection, they will remain standing on their feet until sweat bridles them. They will cry out: 'O Lord, settle our account, even if it be to the Fire!' He said: 'Then Allah will send winds that will strike between them, and a caller will cry out: **وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ**. So He will separate them; the criminals will go to the Fire, and whoever had faith in his heart will go to Paradise.'"

I say: It has been mentioned in some narrations that Allah, the Glorified, manifests Himself to them, and they become occupied with Him to the exclusion of all else as long as the manifestation (التجلي) lasts. What is meant by it is the lifting of every veil between them and their Lord, not visual seeing (الرؤية البصرية), which cannot occur except with directions and dimensions, for these are impossible in His regard, the Exalted.

In the *Iʿtiqadat* of al-Saduq, he (peace be upon him) said: **من أصغى إلى ناطق فقد عبده ـ فإن كان الناطق عن الله فقد عبد الله ، وإن كان الناطق عن إبليس فقد عبد إبليس** — "Whoever listens to a speaker has worshipped him; if the speaker speaks from Allah, he has worshipped Allah, and if the speaker speaks from Iblis, he has worshipped Iblis."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Salim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "The limbs do not bear witness against a believer; they only bear witness against one for whom the word of punishment has become due. As for the believer, he is given his book in his right hand. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَأُولئِكَ يَقْرَؤُنَ كِتابَهُمْ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً** — 'As for those who are given their record in their right hand—they will read their record, and they will not be wronged so much as a peach-pit fiber.'" (17:71).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Mus'adah ibn Sadaqah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his grandfather, he said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said in a sermon describing the horror of the Day of Resurrection: 'Allah will seal the mouths so they do not speak, and the hands will speak, and the feet will bear witness, and the skins will speak of what they did; thus they will not hide any discourse from Allah.'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, some of which will come under the commentary on His saying: "their hearing, their sight, and their skins will bear witness against them" (41:20), and some have already passed in the discussion on His saying: "Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable." (17:36).
